geography: Ngari Prefecture, Tibet Autonomous Region, China. Elevation: 6,638 meters (21,778 feet). Four-sided pyramid shape with snow-covered peaks; considered the most sacred mountain in multiple Asian traditions. Lake Manasarovar at its base
relatedEvents: Parvati's tapasya (austerity) to win Shiva's love
The churning of the cosmic ocean (Mount Mandara role)

keyMyths.title: The Abode of Shiva
keyMyths.description: In Hinduism, Mount Kailash is revered as the dwelling place of Lord Shiva and his consort Parvati. It is believed that Shiva meditates eternally on the summit, making it the center of the universe and a place of immense spiritual power.

keyMyths.title: Meru in Buddhist Cosmology
keyMyths.description: In Buddhist cosmology, Mount Kailash is identified as Mount Meru, the central axis of the world. It is considered the home of various deities and bodhisattvas, and circumambulating it is believed to purify sins and accumulate merit.

keyMyths.title: Swastika Mountain
keyMyths.description: The southern face of Mount Kailash features a prominent vertical fissure intersected by a horizontal rock stratum, creating a swastika symbol. This is considered a sacred symbol in both Hinduism and Buddhism, representing auspiciousness and well-being.
keyMyths.source: Accounts of early Western explorers and Buddhist pilgrims

keyMyths.title: The Legend of Milarepa
keyMyths.description: The Tibetan Buddhist yogi Milarepa is said to have conquered Mount Kailash in a contest of magical powers with a Bon priest. According to legend, Milarepa flew to the summit, establishing Buddhism's dominance over the region.

keyMyths.title: Lake Manasarovar and Rakshastal
keyMyths.description: Adjacent to Mount Kailash are two sacred lakes: Manasarovar and Rakshastal. Manasarovar, representing light and purity, is believed to have been created from Brahma's mind, while Rakshastal, representing darkness and destruction, is associated with Ravana. The duality of these lakes symbolizes the balance of opposing forces in the universe.
keyMyths.source: Hindu and Buddhist scriptures relating to the Kailash region
